behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is real filesystem watch behavior (recursive watch semantics and OS event emission across nested paths). A mocked watcher can miss real recursion/inode/path edge cases and falsely pass (REAL-INFRA and MOCK-HIDE). Rejected lower level: behavior text explicitly depends on files "inside a subdirectory of the watched root," which disqualifies pure Unit/Integration-only wiring tests.
behaviors.test_description: Start the real daemon watching a real temp directory tree; create a nested subdirectory and write a file there; assert one pipeline execution and output artifact for that nested file path.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: Primary failure boundary is module handoff/state propagation: request intake + dedup cache/window + pipeline trigger. No required real OS/network/subprocess for this behavior text; duplicates can be injected in-process. Rejected higher level: no behavior text requiring real filesystem events or subprocesses, and no AI output dependence.
behaviors.test_description: Wire intake, dedup component, clock/time-window config, and pipeline stub in-process; submit same file path twice within the configured window; assert first request is processed and second is dropped, then assert processing resumes after window expiry.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly references OS multi-event emission for one write; this is real OS/filesystem watcher behavior. In-process fakes typically emit idealized events and can hide duplicate-event burst patterns (REAL-INFRA, MOCK-HIDE). Rejected lower level: text "operating system emits multiple filesystem events" disqualifies Unit/Integration as minimum.
behaviors.test_description: Run daemon against a real watched directory, perform a write pattern known to trigger multiple events (e.g., temp-file write + rename or rapid append/close), and assert exactly one processing execution/output for the target file.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is OS filesystem lifecycle/inode changes while a live watcher runs. Real remove/recreate behavior often differs from mocks and is exactly what can break re-subscription logic (REAL-INFRA, MOCK-HIDE). Rejected lower level: behavior text requires directory removal/recreation while running, which is an OS-level condition.
behaviors.test_description: Launch daemon on a real directory, delete the watched directory, recreate it at the same path, then create a new file; assert daemon continues running and processes the post-recreate file without restart.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: Ordering depends on real filesystem metadata (creation timestamps), timestamp resolution, and event timing under concurrent arrivals. A pure in-process fake can mask clock/metadata granularity and event-race failures (REAL-INFRA, MOCK-HIDE). Rejected lower level: behavior text ties ordering to file creation timestamps under simultaneous arrival, which relies on real filesystem semantics.
behaviors.test_description: Using a real watched directory, create multiple files near-simultaneously with controlled timestamp differences where possible; assert observed processing order matches creation-time ordering policy (including deterministic tie-break rule if equal timestamps).

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: This behavior depends on real file-write lifecycle and event timing (partial writes, close/flush semantics). Mocked I/O usually cannot reproduce true in-progress write states and race windows, so failures can be hidden (REAL-INFRA, MOCK-HIDE). Rejected lower level: behavior text explicitly states "still being written" at event time, an OS/filesystem runtime condition.
behaviors.test_description: Run daemon on a real directory; start writing a large file in chunks with delays (keeping the handle open), trigger arrival event, verify daemon does not process mid-write, then verify processing starts only after final flush/close.
